textDisplay:
Just gonna break down this combat round:
0:10 - Barbarian: "Okay, chop it off!" - passes the DC for perception and sleight of hand checks on a loose brick and stealthily picks it up
0:14 - Guard A: rolls less than the Barbarian's AC and misses. Hits brick instead
- - - combat begins and everyone rolls initiative! - - -
0:15 - Surprise round begins! Barbarian rolls to attack and hits! Smashes brick on Guard A's foot and face.
0:16 - Extra Attack! Throws brick to Guard B who is on his way to help. Guard B rolls natural 20 on reflex save, ducks in time and brick smashes into Guard C right behind him.
0:18 - top of initiative! Bard uses turn to try and cut his bonds through the corner of a stone step. Fails dexterity check.
0:19 - Guard C's turn:  rushes towards Barbarian and attacks. Misses!
           Guard A and B use their turns begin to crawl away from the battle.
           Barbarian's turn: succeeds in an athletics check to knock Guard C prone.
0:21 - top of initiative! Bard: fails dex check to break bonds on dull-edged stone step. Uses bonus action to inspire Babarian with "Oh we got em now!!!"
0:23 - Guard C approaches Barbarian cautiously, A and B still attempting to crawl away
           Barbarian uses extra inspiration from Bard to throw herself on Guard C, grabbing his head, and knocking him prone. succeeds both athletics and acrobatics checks!
0:25 - top of initiative! Bard still failing to cut his bonds loose.
textOriginal:
Just gonna break down this combat round:
0:10 - Barbarian: "Okay, chop it off!" - passes the DC for perception and sleight of hand checks on a loose brick and stealthily picks it up
0:14 - Guard A: rolls less than the Barbarian's AC and misses. Hits brick instead
- - - combat begins and everyone rolls initiative! - - -
0:15 - Surprise round begins! Barbarian rolls to attack and hits! Smashes brick on Guard A's foot and face.
0:16 - Extra Attack! Throws brick to Guard B who is on his way to help. Guard B rolls natural 20 on reflex save, ducks in time and brick smashes into Guard C right behind him.

textDisplay:
Hopefully they don't make Szass Tam out to be a Scooby Doo villain in this movie. If you've ever read the Forgotten Realms' "Haunted Lands" series, then you know he's not only immensely powerful, but he's also insanely genius, unbelievably tough, as well as easily the most ambitious villain in the entire Forgotten Realms' history (just look up the "Ritual of Unmaking", makes Karsus' and Larloch's ambition pale in comparison).

Although, judging by what i see at 0:37, i won't get my hopes up. The directors obviously don't know that Szass Tam had all the flesh blasted off his bones and his bones blackened and charred by Mirror's (a spectral Paladin of Amaunator) Holy Explosion centered within Szass' ribcage. That attack would've wiped out of existence like 99% of Liches (it wiped out Mirror), but Szass Tam survived it and continued fighting, and won.
textOriginal:
Hopefully they don't make Szass Tam out to be a Scooby Doo villain in this movie. If you've ever read the Forgotten Realms' "Haunted Lands" series, then you know he's not only immensely powerful, but he's also insanely genius, unbelievably tough, as well as easily the most ambitious villain in the entire Forgotten Realms' history (just look up the "Ritual of Unmaking", makes Karsus' and Larloch's ambition pale in comparison).

textDisplay:
It's D&D.  Adventurers are super-humans with character levels and guards are not, so the characters' classes have more to do with their physical abilities than their sex.  You're right about movies presumably set in a world like ours, that being the case, but in the world of D&D, they have a different, but still consistent set of rules that makes it internally consistent.  
PC Barbarian > Nameless NPC Guards.

textDisplay:
@Defectivegg Funny you mention Marvel movies, because a lot of them have been suffering from the same problem this movie seems to be: Lack of serious scenes, and thus just being a string of scenes either referencing memes or trying to become memes.
Actual D&D games are going to have levity-they usually need it-but a game where everyone took it this lightly is only good for a one-shot, because it's not going to last much further. It's a basic rule of storytelling: If nobody cares about the stakes, the story is going to be dead in the water.
textOriginal:
@Defectivegg Funny you mention Marvel movies, because a lot of them have been suffering from the same problem this movie seems to be: Lack of serious scenes, and thus just being a string of scenes either referencing memes or trying to become memes.
Actual D&D games are going to have levity-they usually need it-but a game where everyone took it this lightly is only good for a one-shot, because it's not going to last much further. It's a basic rule of storytelling: If nobody cares about the stakes, the story is going to be dead in the water.

textDisplay:
I can definitely see a good franchise built around D&D if it appears to be made with as much love and care as this film seems to be, albeit not with a grander mythology being built up like the MCU. Preferably the next installments would be standalone sequels taking place in different realms with a different cast of different characters. Maybe they all take place in the same world, the characters just don't intersect.
Who would you like to see in a D&D film?

textDisplay:
How this movie came to be:

Paramount Exec 1: Hey, we want to do something like Guardians of the Galaxy.

Exec 2: Hmmm...maybe we do something sci-fi?

Exec 1: Nah, we have to do something different enough where people won't notice and call us out for it. 

Exec 2: Hmmm...well, we do have Dungeons and Dragons. That's fantasy. That's different enough, right?

Exec 1: Yes! Greenlight immediately with a big budget! And get the hottest, most expensive stars to be in it!
textOriginal:
How this movie came to be:

Paramount Exec 1: Hey, we want to do something like Guardians of the Galaxy.

Exec 2: Hmmm...maybe we do something sci-fi?

Exec 1: Nah, we have to do something different enough where people won't notice and call us out for it. 

Exec 2: Hmmm...well, we do have Dungeons and Dragons. That's fantasy. That's different enough, right?

Exec 1: Yes! Greenlight immediately with a big budget! And get the hottest, most expensive stars to be in it!

textDisplay:
I was planning on going to the theater and seeing this but Hasbro/WotC (the company that owns the D&D brand) has totally ruined it for me with their OGL nonsense.

What can they do to get me to go see it?  Answer's pretty simple.
Keep OGL 1.0a in place and recognize it's irrevocable.
Admit OGL 1.1 wasn't a draft.
Scrap OGL 1.2 (or 2.0..whatever they've decided to call it.  Name seems to change daily)
Fire Cynthia Williams.
Sign the ORC (the OGL being developed by Paizo and about 1500 other companies that will be held by a trust, not a company).

Otherwise I won't go see it and I won't buy the DVD/Blu-ray.   Instead, I'll speak to some co-workers who know how to ..acquire.. movies.

#openDnD
textOriginal:

Fire Cynthia Williams.
Sign the ORC (the OGL being developed by Paizo and about 1500 other companies that will be held by a trust, not a company).

Otherwise I won't go see it and I won't buy the DVD/Blu-ray.   Instead, I'll speak to some co-workers who know how to ..acquire.. movies.

#openDnD

textDisplay:
Wow, this sure does look good!

Pity thanks to Cynthia Williams and Chris Cao it's likely to underperform compared to what it could have done. If I were Chris Pine, or all of the actors really, I'd be considering a lawsuit against executives so incredibly bad at very basic aspects of business that they hamstrung a project I put my time and effort into *two months* before it was due to come out.

Don't see this. Under any circumstances. The executives behind Wizards and Hasbro right now have to be made to suffer if we want to have a D&D *worth* having in the future.

textDisplay:
I could be wrong, and I hope I am, but it looks like it suffers from the same problem every other DnD movie ever has.

It doesn't resemble DnD.

I mean, it looks like great fantasy, and it DOES have the feel of a table of players behind it, but it is lacking certain things that it needs to resemble DnD.

The big one, IMO, is the magic - that does not resemble DnD magic in any way.  I see no component usage, except maybe somatic, and I am willing to bet you money that they just ignore the memorization of spells thing.  The way magic works is perhaps the most distinct part of DnD.

Also, is it just me, or does it seem like every time they try to do a DnD movie, it's a party of nothing but thieves?  I mean, sure, in some DnD games all the characters steal everything they can, but they're not all thieves... some are bards!

At least they're showing a few of the D&D setting monsters and stuff in this, so maybe I'm wrong, but I think the monsters were lower hanging fruit.

Does it look like a good fantasy movie?  Heck, yeah.  Does it look like a DnD movie?  Not to me.  This is, of course, just my opinion, and you are encouraged to hold a different one - I do not intend to join a fight over this opinion.

The big one, IMO, is the magic - that does not resemble DnD magic in any way.  I see no component usage, except maybe somatic, and I am willing to bet you money that they just ignore the memorization of spells thing.  The way magic works is perhaps the most distinct part of DnD.

Also, is it just me, or does it seem like every time they try to do a DnD movie, it's a party of nothing but thieves?  I mean, sure, in some DnD games all the characters steal everything they can, but they're not all thieves... some are bards!

At least they're showing a few of the D&D setting monsters and stuff in this, so maybe I'm wrong, but I think the monsters were lower hanging fruit.

Does it look like a good fantasy movie?  Heck, yeah.  Does it look like a DnD movie?  Not to me.  This is, of course, just my opinion, and you are encouraged to hold a different one - I do not intend to join a fight over this opinion.

textDisplay:
Dude, being excited about a movie where the rights are owned by a shitty corporation isn't me being a shill. I like the actors, I like the concept, and from what they've shown they let everyone have fun with it & they don't take it too seriously. 
Just because you're consumed by the misery of one of your favourite ttrpgs being hijacked by corporate greed doesn't mean other people can't enjoy things. Go get a snack or something, and be prepared for a wave of new fans who need help navigating the game when they decide to play because of this movie.

textDisplay:
If you love D&D you won't go see this movie. WOTC need to know how bad they messed up. 
If you are a fan of good cinema, then DEFINITELY DON'T go see the dumpster fire this looks like. I'm seeing a lot of comments on here that this looks like how most D&D games actually go. From the trailer I'd agree; snappy one liners at each other, characters doing stupid stuff while the DM tries to be serious, but I don't know if I want to watch that for 2 hours. Also it has that cosplay look to it. Everyone is too clean and well kept for a medieval setting like the Forgotten Realms. Where do they get all the hair gel? This looks bad. 
Why not do a serious adaptation of Drizzt's novels, the Dragonlance trilogy, or Ravenloft? 
I won't be seeing this mess.

textDisplay:
Romans 3:23 states: "For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God;". We are all sinners, that have committed at least one sin in our lives, which earns us an eternal punishment in Hell. Revelation states 21:8 "But the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars, shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death.". God sent his Son, Jesus, to Earth as a perfect sinless sacrifice. To take on all the sins of the world; both past, present, and future sins. Shedding his blood on the cross as a blood atonement for all sins. After dying on the cross, he rose from the dead on the 3rd day and rose to Heaven. To show life after death. To show everlasting life through Jesus's sacrifice. John 14:6 states: "Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me.". Romans 10:13 states "For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.". 

   Eternal life in heaven is just one prayer away. All you have to do is believe that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, died on the cross for your sins. As an atonement for your sins. And rose from the grave on the 3rd day. Ask Jesus to save you of your sins and give you eternal life in Heaven. Salvation is a free and everlasting gift from God. Please don't let pride drag you into eternal punishment in Hell, but ask Jesus to give you eternal life in Heaven. If you're getting a strong feeling of resistance while reading this, it's because Satan doesn't want you to get saved. He wants you to resist, and think it's not real. He wants you in Hell for all eternity with him. Hell is not a fun place, like worldly people like to imagine. It's everlasting torture with no relief. I'm only taking the time to text this, because I want to get the message out. There are so many distractions in life keeping people from witnessing to other people. Keeping people from thinking about salvation. Keeping you from thinking about life after death. Please, ask Jesus into your hearts to save you of your sins and cleanse you from all unrighteousness. Ask him for a place in Heaven, and he will save you from eternal damnation in Hell.

textDisplay: Yeah, too campy for me. It's hard to get the humor right and have the movie maintain immersion/authenticity. Similar to the Willow series, it feels like they reaching too hard for a laugh. The all Led Zeppelin soundtrack completely ruins the authenticity for me, and some of those props are just hilariously phoney. That axe looks like it should weigh about 30 lbs but she whips it around like it weighs 2 lbs. lol!  I agree with you, a dark fantasy with some well-placed dark humor would be amazing. They're playing to a certain audience and it's not old school die-hard D&D players from the 80's. Oh well, maybe next D&D movie it'll happen...
textOriginal: Yeah, too campy for me. It's hard to get the humor right and have the movie maintain immersion/authenticity. Similar to the Willow series, it feels like they reaching too hard for a laugh. The all Led Zeppelin soundtrack completely ruins the authenticity for me, and some of those props are just hilariously phoney. That axe looks like it should weigh about 30 lbs but she whips it around like it weighs 2 lbs. lol!  I agree with you, a dark fantasy with some well-placed dark humor would be amazing. They're playing to a certain audience and it's not old school die-hard D&D players from the 80's. Oh well, maybe next D&D movie it'll happen...

textDisplay:
We have full-length Forgotten Realms story with iconic characters and even more renown face of evil. This lich over there is Szass Tam himself. Shots taken on Iceland seem to show Icewind Dale. Main part probably will take place somewhere near the Sea of Fallen Stars but I am not sure, didn't find any info. Still I am pretty excited with Red Wizards as they showed up in my own campaign too. Monsters and effects are in pretty good shape. Maybe it's not the highest league, but I am quite certain about good fun. 
And yeah, they didn't film Forgotten Realms story. It's obvious since story doesn't evolve around Elminster, Artemis, Seven Sisters, any of main heroes of this world. It's the roleplaying game adapted, so it's supposed to be goofy ;)
textOriginal:
We have full-length Forgotten Realms story with iconic characters and even more renown face of evil. This lich over there is Szass Tam himself. Shots taken on Iceland seem to show Icewind Dale. Main part probably will take place somewhere near the Sea of Fallen Stars but I am not sure, didn't find any info. Still I am pretty excited with Red Wizards as they showed up in my own campaign too. Monsters and effects are in pretty good shape. Maybe it's not the highest league, but I am quite certain about good fun. 
And yeah, they didn't film Forgotten Realms story. It's obvious since story doesn't evolve around Elminster, Artemis, Seven Sisters, any of main heroes of this world. It's the roleplaying game adapted, so it's supposed to be goofy ;)

textDisplay: Quick reminder to those who are boycotting this film because of the OGL; Those working on this film are completely separate from the bigwigs trying to kill d&d homebrew, and not seeing this movie will do very little to change the current situation and put a lot of people who are not to blame out of money. Not to mention even if it did matter, we should reward companies for putting out good content and if Hasbro/WoTC sees this movie succeed and the OGL fail, then it will send a clear message as to what this community wants and expect from them. Do what you want with your money obviously but just keep this in mind :)
textOriginal: Quick reminder to those who are boycotting this film because of the OGL; Those working on this film are completely separate from the bigwigs trying to kill d&d homebrew, and not seeing this movie will do very little to change the current situation and put a lot of people who are not to blame out of money. textDisplay:
Once upon a time, there were heroes. And kids who played and dreamed of becoming heroes. And there was fantasy, there was mystery, fear of evil, the unbelievable and the unexpected. And those youngsters were formed as adults on this imagery that accustomed them to think creatively and revolutionarily.
Once upon a time.
Now you get little puppets doing predictable stunts and talking as they would sitting in front of an ice-cream parlour. And it is normal that there are no longer kids who dream of becoming special and living dreams.
Someone out there is plucking the hunger for imagination and creativity out of young humans, which means eradicating from the human being the thing that brought them out of the cave to explore the world.
What a disaster.
What a blame.
textOriginal:
Once upon a time, there were heroes. And kids who played and dreamed of becoming heroes. And there was fantasy, there was mystery, fear of evil, the unbelievable and the unexpected. And those youngsters were formed as adults on this imagery that accustomed them to think creatively and revolutionarily.
Once upon a time.
Now you get little puppets doing predictable stunts and talking as they would sitting in front of an ice-cream parlour. And it is normal that there are no longer kids who dream of becoming special and living dreams.
Someone out there is plucking the hunger for imagination and creativity out of young humans, which means eradicating from the human being the thing that brought them out of the cave to explore the world.
What a disaster.
What a blame.

textDisplay: @peterstoric A good example would be Netflix’s Stranger Things. Those were ordinary kids playing the game. In terms of character renditions, the movie Conan the Barbarian was closer to what most people thought about. So ideally a D&D movie would be a fusion of those 2. Instead this movie seems to be a mashup of a D&D video game blended in with the worst parts of Indiana Jones Temple of Doom.

textDisplay:
Who is this even for? God damn, I feel like a lot of these comments are from people who have only ever played dnd once in their life.

This looks no different from any other dumb, forgettable Holleywood schlock with a coat of DnD paint on it to try to appeal to nerds. It looks unfunny, unoriginal, and uninspired, while trying to pander to people who are most likely to notice how cynical it is. It looks like a bad Marvel movie, for fucks sake.

“Oh dnd is kinda popular these days, let’s make Unfunny Fantasy Comedy Blockbuster #1837491 and throw in some terrible CG to look like dnd monsters and call it a day.”

I have no doubt this will just print money at the box office but it’s pretty disheartening that it seems like no one in these comments has any standards for the movies they watch.
textOriginal:
Who is this even for? God damn, I feel like a lot of these comments are from people who have only ever played dnd once in their life.

This looks no different from any other dumb, forgettable Holleywood schlock with a coat of DnD paint on it to try to appeal to nerds. It looks unfunny, unoriginal, and uninspired, while trying to pander to people who are most likely to notice how cynical it is. It looks like a bad Marvel movie, for fucks sake.

“Oh dnd is kinda popular these days, let’s make Unfunny Fantasy Comedy Blockbuster #1837491 and throw in some terrible CG to look like dnd monsters and call it a day.”

I have no doubt this will just print money at the box office but it’s pretty disheartening that it seems like no one in these comments has any standards for the movies they watch.

textDisplay:
Ok, hmmm, I seem to see a lot of comments on here leaning to one extreme or the other. For those that support hasbro, it is every individuals right to vote with their wallet. If they don’t want to throw it away to a corporation that could truly not care a rip about their fanbase, then that is up to them. Hasbro and WOTC has a fiduciary interest to keep the money flowing to satisfy their investors. I fear they have played a short game and are going toward short money.

For the fanbase that is up in arms, what happens if your boycott succeeds? Do you want it to be another twenty years until someone tries to make a movie or a show again? As a kid, I was heartbroken when the cartoon was canceled without any finality. Then the first movie came out. A small and unlikable reprieve. Followed by a bad sequel and a hokey but passable third movie. If the movie fails because of the boycott, will the show be cancelled too? And what of Vox Machina? If Critical Role sells out to WOTC, will you stop watching that as well until it gets cancelled? I personally like that show and it seems like a waste if it were to go by the wayside. Maybe let’s all take a step back and take breath and figure out a real way forward

textDisplay:
If you’re a D&D fan who is thinking about boycotting this film because of the OGL thing, just remember that if the movie doesn’t make enough money we (the fans) will never get another Dungeons & Dragons movie.
If it is a blockbuster hit we will get a sequel, maybe even a franchise out of it. Plus it could open the door for more fantasy films by other creators (Pathfinder, etc.) Look at what the first Iron Man did for the genre of superhero movies.

textDisplay: The story is in the Manual. If I played “Hungry Hungry Hippos” with my friends in a deathly serious way, is it right to assume that everyone else did the same and therefore the movie must be that way? Stop saying that making this thing “superdumb” was the “right thing” because that’s how you played D&D with your friends, I don’t believe you did, specially with those $u$piciou$ly $hort comments and the pronouns in your profiles that are used by people that hate nerd stuff. Just like any other board game, everyone has a different experience every single time you played it, even if you do it with the same group of friends. Movies are meant to be for the general audience that didn’t played them exactly like you did, and quite probably didn’t even played it at all. BUT there is one element that is totally objective and every single person that ever played the game experienced exactly the same way as everyone else, The MANUAL, The MANUAL which is not subjective as your defense is. Every detail about the characters and their world is in the manual and is written seriously, there is no goofy “comedy” in it all. What they are doing here is mocking the seriousness of the source material from their very subjective and narcissistic point of view. If they had followed the manual instead, or the books, or comics that were written afterwards, they could had filled the expectations of the biggest part of the audience. That’s why your statement is utter stupid. You still want things that don’t belong to you, to reflect you, because you are incapable to like them the way they originally are. Narcissist.

textDisplay: The story is in the Manual. If I played “Hungry Hungry Hippos” with my friends in a deathly serious way, is it right to assume that everyone else did the same and therefore the movie must be that way? Stop saying that making this thing “superdumb” was the “right thing” because that’s how you played D&D with your friends, I don’t believe you did, specially with those suspiciously $hort comments and the pronouns in your profiles that are used by people that hate nerd stuff. Just like any other board game, everyone has a different experience every single time you played it, even if you do it with the same group of friends. Movies are meant to be for the general audience that didn’t played them exactly like you did, and quite probably didn’t even played it at all. BUT there is one element that is totally objective and every single person that ever played the game experienced exactly the same way as everyone else, The MANUAL, The MANUAL which is not subjective as your defense is. Every detail about the characters and their world is in the manual and is written seriously, there is no goofy “comedy” in it all. What they are doing here is mocking the seriousness of the source material from their very subjective and narcissistic point of view. If they had followed the manual instead, or the books, or comics that were written afterwards, they could had filled the expectations of the biggest part of the audience. That’s why your statement is utter stupid. You still want things that don’e belong to you, to reflect you.

Also why specifically 3.0e? Most boomers who can't move on are either diehard AD&D 2e THAC0 purists who reject any WotC material or 3.5e fans because 3.5e is better and there are still some people playing it occasionally, the ones who haven't moved to Pathfinder yet for some reason.
textOriginal:
@steveross8364 "Ah yes, I can distinctly recognize that based on a short sequence this movie uses 5e rules for grappling creatures and not the obviously superior 3e."

Also why specifically 3.0e? Most boomers who can't move on are either diehard AD&D 2e THAC0 purists who reject any WotC material or 3.5e fans because 3.5e is better and there are still some people playing it occasionally, the ones who haven't moved to Pathfinder yet for some reason.

textDisplay: @neelot9842 Sounding a lot like a 5e fanboi there Nee.  AD&D was not very good, even for it's time, other RPGs were far superior.  Even the D&D basic set that predated it wasn't that good either but when WotC did 3rd edition, that was a vast improvement.  So 3.0 found a place and was a good workable system.  3.0 and 3.5 were very close with a few rule changes and clarifications.  The changes made since 3.5 have been backward steps (with only a couple of good ideas and tons of bad ones in 5e) IMHO.  As for "why 3.0?"  I guess it's because it was a good version with lots of supporting/add-on manuals and massive in-depth Campaign settings.  If 5e had been as big an advance as 3.0 had been from AD&D I would've happily 'moved on' as you put it but why move on to an inferior product?  Oh yes, because everyone else is using it despite it being so inferior.  Now 'One D&D' is coming. I'm not optimistic from what I've heard so far...
textOriginal: @neelot9842 Sounding a lot like a 5e fanboi there Nee.  AD&D was not very good, even for it's time, other RPGs were far superior.  Even the D&D basic set that predated it wasn't that good either but when WotC did 3rd edition, that was a vast improvement.  So 3.0 found a place and was a good workable system.  3.0 and 3.5 were very close with a few rule changes and clarifications.  The changes made since 3.5 have been backward steps (with only a couple of good ideas and tons of bad ones in 5e) IMHO.  As for "why 3.0?"  I guess it's because it was a good version with lots of supporting/add-on manuals and massive in-depth Campaign settings.  If 5e had been as big an advance as 3.0 had been from AD&D I would've happily 'moved on' as you put it but why move on to an inferior product?  Oh yes, because everyone else is using it despite it being so inferior.  Now 'One D&D' is coming. I'm not optimistic from what I've heard so far...

textDisplay: @steveross8364 I'm just not weird about my hobby. 5e isn't even the first system I messed around with (that would be VtM Revised) I mostly run 5e because it's easier to understand, significantly faster to run, do all the math on the fly and there are plenty of enthusiastic new players willing to get into it without any previous tabletop RPG experience. That being said with the recent OGL fiasco, I've actually been keen on introducing my more experienced players to PF2e.
textOriginal: @steveross8364 I'm just not weird about my hobby. 5e isn't even the first system I messed around with (that would be VtM Revised) I mostly run 5e because it's easier to understand, significantly faster to run, do all the math on the fly and there are plenty of enthusiastic new players willing to get into it without any previous tabletop RPG experience. That being said with the recent OGL fiasco, I've actually been keen on introducing my more experienced players to PF2e.

textDisplay: I was going to be excited about this movie, but I'm just coming here to point out that after the past month of WoTC BS over the OGL, I hope it crashes and burns, which is really unfortunate for the writers, actors, production crew, and the fans who would've wanted to see more (fortunately for everyone before the fans, they at least already got paid, but who doesn't want to see something they worked on do well), but it's still time to let WoTC know that until they GTFO their BS over the OGL, we keep voting with our wallets.  Keep cancelling those D&D Beyond Subs and provide feedback they need to stay off the VTTs and commit to a true OGL.  If they want to protect TV and Movie IPs fine.  If they want to monetize their own VTT fine.  Just bring it to market and make it a better product and use the creators who work with you to promote it and improve it.  This Dickensian stuff of subjecting small and large content creators or squeezing them out has got to end.  Or just go ORC!
textOriginal: I was going to be excited about this movie, but I'm just coming here to point out that after the past month of WoTC BS over the OGL, I hope it crashes and burns, which is really unfortunate for the writers, actors, production crew, and the fans who would've wanted to see more (fortunately for everyone before the fans, they at least already got paid, but who doesn't want to see something they worked on do well), but it's still time to let WoTC know that until they GTFO their BS over the OGL, we keep voting with our wallets.  Keep cancelling those D&D Beyond Subs and provide feedback they need to stay off the VTTs and commit to a true OGL.  If they want to protect TV and Movie IPs fine.  If they want to monetize their own VTT fine.  Just bring it to market and make it a better product and use the creators who work with you to promote it and improve it.  This Dickensian stuff of subjecting small and large content creators or squeezing them out has got to end.  Or just go ORC!

textDisplay:
It's a shame that Wizards of the Coast decided to attack the gaming industry and their partners by breaking over 20 years of promises about their Open Gaming License. Can't do good things for movie viewing when a significant and vocal portion of your target audience wants nothing to do with anything licensed from Hasbro or Wizards of the Coast. I feel sad for their partners and licensees having to deal with such idiocy. Not to mention the producers, directors, and cast not deserving it.
Tortious Interference, anybody? It isn't like Wizards hasn't done this before and didn't know there would be a backlash.
textOriginal:
It's a shame that Wizards of the Coast decided to attack the gaming industry and their partners by breaking over 20 years of promises about their Open Gaming License. Can't do good things for movie viewing when a significant and vocal portion of your target audience wants nothing to do with anything licensed from Hasbro or Wizards of the Coast. I feel sad for their partners and licensees having to deal with such idiocy. Not to mention the producers, directors, and cast not deserving it.
Tortious Interference, anybody? It isn't like Wizards hasn't done this before and didn't know there would be a backlash.

textDisplay:
Y'all I don't know...
If I want silliness in the midst of my immersive fantasy world I already got Pippin Took.  I mean, it's possible to do silly and goofy and still not take me out of the story with the contempo dialogue and all that.  Then again we are talking about a fantasy world.  
But for silliness I think it's more effective when Alan Rickman says "And Cancel Christmas!" in RH: Prince of Thieves because it's so over the top, he's fully invested in delivering that line because it's so on point for his character in the story, and it makes sense in context because Christmas is a thing in that world.  The original "Willow" was good too - even though Val Kilmer delivered with his American Accent, he'd still say things that made contextual sense AND were funny "I don't love her! She kicked me in the face!" so it doesn't take me out of the movie.
All i"m getting so far from these trailers is OTT visuals, a stew pot of American accents, wry Britishness, and the sense that every line is being delivered with a wink and a nod.  Maybe that's how the people play D & D these days, I guess.
textOriginal:
Y'all I don't know...
If I want silliness in the midst of my immersive fantasy world I already got Pippin Took.  I mean, it's possible to do silly and goofy and still not take me out of the story with the contempo dialogue and all that.  Then again we are talking about a fantasy world.  
But for silliness I think it's more effective when Alan Rickman says "And Cancel Christmas!" in RH: Prince of Thieves because it's so over the top, he's fully invested in delivering that line because it's so on point for his character in the story, and it makes sense in context because Christmas is a thing in that world.  The original "Willow" was good too - even though Val Kilmer delivered with his American Accent, he'd still say things that made contextual sense AND were funny "I don't love her! She kicked me in the face!" so it doesn't take me out of the movie.
All i"m getting so far from these trailers is OTT visuals, a stew pot of American accents, wry Britishness, and the sense that every line is being delivered with a wink and a nod.  Maybe that's how the people play D & D these days, I guess.

textDisplay:
it is a lot of fun. I started during the pandemic and if you know someone who plays ask them to introduce you to join a session. Everyone in the community (save for some assholes) love introducing the world to new players. You can craft a character in an hour or less and join some friends for a short adventure and see if you like it.

The hardest part is scheduling. The rules and lore is quite simple once you get the mechanics understood.
textOriginal:
it is a lot of fun. I started during the pandemic and if you know someone who plays ask them to introduce you to join a session. The hardest part is scheduling. The rules and lore is quite simple once you get the mechanics understood.

SPOILERS
